steps.prompt: Which is the main finite verb?

hints.intellegerent: 'Intellegerent' is a finite verb but in the subordinate purpose clause after 'ut'
hints.sensum: 'Sensum' is a noun meaning 'understanding/mind' in the accusative
hints.scripturas: 'Scripturas' is a noun meaning 'scriptures' in the accusative

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'aperit' — 'aperuit' with -uit is perfect
hints.perfect subjunctive: Perfect subjunctive would be 'aperuerit' — 'aperuit' is indicative

hints.appareo, apparere, apparui, apparitum: These are the parts of 'appareo' (to appear)
hints.paro, parare, paravi, paratum: These are the parts of 'paro' (to prepare)
hints.operio, operire, operui, opertum: These are the parts of 'operio' (to cover) — the opposite of 'aperire'

steps.reveal: ut intellegerent = so that they might understand (purpose clause)

hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect indicative would be 'intellegebant' — '-erent' marks subjunctive
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'intellegant' — 'intellegerent' is imperfect
hints.pluperfect subjunctive: Pluperfect subjunctive would be 'intellexissent' — 'intellegerent' is imperfect

hints.lego, legere, legi, lectum: These are the parts of 'lego' (to read/choose) — 'intellegere' is inter + legere
hints.neglego, neglegere, neglexi, neglectum: These are the parts of 'neglego' (to neglect) — a different compound of 'legere'
hints.eligo, eligere, elegi, electum: These are the parts of 'eligo' (to choose/elect)

steps.target: sequence of tenses: primary verb 'aperuit' (historical perfect) takes secondary sequence
choices: sequence of tenses: primary verb 'aperuit' (historical perfect) takes secondary sequence
the purpose is in the past
steps.reveal: After a historical perfect, 'ut' purpose clauses use imperfect subjunctive (secondary sequence)

hints.the purpose is in the past: Purpose clauses express intent, not past time — the imperfect is required by sequence of tenses
hints.it is an unreal condition: Unreal conditions use 'si' — this is a purpose clause with 'ut'
hints.it expresses repeated action: Imperfect subjunctive in purpose clauses reflects secondary sequence, not repeated action

steps.reveal: aperuit illis sensum = he opened their understanding (dat. of advantage)

hints.ablative: 'Illis' can be ablative, but 'aperuit' + dative = opened to/for them

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'sensus' — 'sensum' with -um is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'sensus' has genitive in -us
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'sensus' has genitive in -us
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'sensus' has genitive in -us

steps.reveal: scripturas = the scriptures (acc. pl., direct object of 'intellegerent')

hints.nominative plural: Nominative plural would be 'scripturae' — 'scripturas' with -as is accusative
hints.genitive singular: Genitive singular would be 'scripturae' — 'scripturas' ends in -as (acc. pl.)
hints.ablative plural: Ablative plural would be 'scripturis' — 'scripturas' is accusative

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'scripturae' ends in -ae
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'scripturae' ends in -ae
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'scripturae' ends in -ae

translation: Then he opened their minds so that they might understand the scriptures
